cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 64-year-old on haemodialysis presents lethargic and nauseated after missing recent dialysis sessions.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~26.0 (+/-2); measured 25.8 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 134 - (96 + 12) = 26, which is raised. This pattern is most consistent with renal failure causing uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: Simple respiratory alkalosis
cases.clinical_stem: 28-year-old presents with rapid breathing and light-headedness while visibly distressed.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. For acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~22.4; measured 22.3 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 12.0. This pattern is consistent with simple hyperventilation.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 29-year-old presents with light-headedness and dehydration after several days of gastroenteritis symptoms.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~38.0 (+/-2); measured 36.2 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 137 - (109 + 20) = 8, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: Opioid toxicity (acute respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 27-year-old presents with reduced consciousness, shallow respirations, and pinpoint pupils.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis due to hypoventilation with appropriate compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~26.1; measured 25.6 is appropriate acute compensation. This pattern fits acute hypoventilation, such as opioid toxicity.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 79-year-old on regular frusemide presents with light-headedness and recent poor oral intake after a small increase in diuretic use.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~44.9; measured 44.8 is appropriate. Anion gap is 141 - (102 + 31) = 8, which is normal. This pattern is most consistent with diuretic-associated contraction alkalosis from diuretic use.

cases.title: Simple respiratory alkalosis
cases.clinical_stem: 29-year-old presents with rapid breathing, light-headedness, and tingling around the mouth during acute distress.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. For acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~21.6; measured 21.9 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 10.0. This pattern is consistent with simple hyperventilation.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 68-year-old taking loop diuretics presents with postural symptoms, dry mucous membranes, and fatigue after recent volume depletion.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~46.3; measured 46.6 is appropriate. Anion gap is 135 - (90 + 33) = 12, which is normal. This pattern is most consistent with diuretic-associated contraction alkalosis from diuretic use.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 69-year-old taking loop diuretics reports postural dizziness and fatigue after several days of reduced intake.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~44.9; measured 44.9 is appropriate. Anion gap is 142 - (101 + 31) = 10, which is normal. This pattern is most consistent with diuretic-associated contraction alkalosis from diuretic use.

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 30-year-old presents with rapid breathing, light-headedness, and perioral tingling after an acute stressful event.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~21.0; measured 20.9 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: 27-year-old presents with nausea, hyperventilation, and tinnitus after ingesting a large quantity of tablets.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (20.0) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 72-year-old with longstanding breathlessness presents with increased sputum and fatigue.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~34.4; measured 34.7 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: COPD exacerbations cause acute rises in CO2 on a background of chronic respiratory acidosis.

Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~38.0 (+/-2); measured 36.0 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 136 - (106 + 20) = 10, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: 21-year-old presents with tinnitus, vomiting, fever, and rapid breathing after an overdose.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (27.0) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~27.5 (+/-2); measured 28.0 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 134 - (93 + 13) = 28 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 72-year-old with heart failure reports cramps and light-headedness after escalating loop diuretics for ankle swelling.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~45.6; measured 44.2 is appropriate. Anion gap is 138 - (95 + 32) = 11, which is normal. This pattern is most consistent with diuretic-associated contraction alkalosis from diuretic use.
